comments.author: @playtime-Chen
comments.text: During its invasion of China and across Asia, the Imperial Japanese Army committed extremely serious crimes against humanity, primarily including biological warfare units, chemical warfare units, large-scale massacre incidents, the "comfort women" system, the "Three Alls Policy" (Burn All, Kill All, Loot All), forced labor systems, and mass graves. I. Biological Warfare Units The Japanese army established multiple complete biological warfare units: Unit 731 (Kanto Army Epidemic Prevention and Water Supply Department), headquartered in Pingfang District, Harbin, which was the largest and most core biological warfare unit; Unit 100 (Kanto Army Veterinary Epidemic Prevention and Water Supply Department), headquartered in Changchun, focusing on spreading bacteria through livestock and plants; Unit 1644 (Central China Expeditionary Army Epidemic Prevention and Water Supply Department), headquartered in Nanjing, primarily covering East and Central China; Unit 8604 (South China Expeditionary Army Epidemic Prevention and Water Supply Department), headquartered in Guangzhou, covering South China; Unit 1855 (North China Branch Unit 1855, North China Expeditionary Army Epidemic Prevention and Water Supply Department), headquartered in Beijing (then known as Beiping), covering North China; Unit 9420 (Southern Army Epidemic Prevention and Water Supply Department), headquartered in Singapore, used for the Southeast Asian battlefield; Unit 691, located in the Jinan area; Unit 543, located in Sunwu County, Heilongjiang Province; Unit 773, located in Mudanjiang; and Unit 319, located in Dalian. In addition, there were: Unit 252 (Beijing), Unit 531 (Mudan Hailin), Hailar Detachment, Linkou Detachment, Sunwu Detachment, as well as dispatched offices in Jinan, Qingdao, Tianjin, Hankou, Jiujiang, Yueyang, and other locations. Unit 1644 (also known as Unit Ei 1644), Unit 2646, Unit 8604 (also known as Unit Ha 8604), and Unit 200 also belonged to this system. II. Chemical Warfare Units Unit 516 (Chemical Warfare Unit), located in Qiqihar, specialized in the research and use of poison gas weapons and was as notorious as Unit 731. The Japanese army extensively used chemical weapons such as mustard gas, Lewisite, and cyanide gas on the Chinese battlefield, particularly in Shanxi, Hubei, Hunan, Yunnan, and other provinces. III. The "Three Alls Policy" (Burn All, Kill All, Loot All) This was one of the most representative systematic atrocities during the invasion of China. In Japanese, it was called "Sankō Sakusen" (Three Alls Campaign) or "Jinmetsu Sakusen" (Annihilation Campaign). It was implemented over a long period by the Japanese North China Area Army, primarily in Hebei, Shanxi, Shandong, Henan, and other provinces. Countless villages were completely burned to the ground, and civilians were subjected to mass slaughter. The number of deaths in North China alone was extremely large. IV. The "Comfort Women" System This was an organized and institutionalized system of sexual slavery implemented by the Japanese military. The regions involved included China, the Korean Peninsula, the Philippines, Indonesia, Burma (Myanmar), Malaya, the Dutch East Indies, East Timor, and others. Academic estimates place the number of victims at over 200,000. V. Large-Scale Massacre Incidents (Within China) Major incidents include: Port Arthur Massacre (November 21, 1894); Jinan Massacre (May 3, 1928); Hailar Massacre (1932); Pingdingshan Massacre (September 16, 1932); Zhenjiang Massacre (December 8, 1937); Nanjing Massacre (December 13, 1937); Wuxi Massacre (November 1937); Changzhou Massacre (November 29, 1937); Suzhou Massacre (November 19, 1937); Jiangyin Massacre (December 2, 1937); Wuhu Massacre (December 10, 1937); Hangzhou Massacre (December 24, 1937); Yangzhou Massacre (December 14, 1937); Xuzhou Massacre (May 20, 1938); Yancheng Massacre (April 26, 1938); Baoshan Tragedy (September 1937); Shantou Massacre (June 1939); Xinyang Massacre (October 1938); Nanchang Massacre (March 27, 1939); Changde Bacteriological Warfare Attack (November 1941); Panjiayu Massacre (January 25, 1941); Beituan Massacre (May 27, 1942); Changjiao Massacre (May 9–12, 1943); Chongqing Bombing (February 18, 1938 – August 23, 1943); Panjiadaizhuang Massacre (December 5, 1942). Additional incidents include: Zhaogezhuang Massacre (October 1937); Xinkou Massacre (October 1937); Fansi Massacre (October 1937); Yangqu Massacre (November 1937); Linyi Massacre (April 1938); Fenghuangshan Massacre (June 1939); Macheng Massacre (September 1939); Cixi Massacre (April 1941); Wuyuan Massacre (1944). Furthermore, there were: Pingyang Massacre (Shanxi), Huangyadong Massacre, large-scale mop-up operations in the Langya Mountain area, the "depopulated zone" policy in Eastern Hebei, and the large "mopping-up" campaign in the Jin-Cha-Ji border region. The Ningbo Plague Attack (1940) and the Quzhou Bacteriological Warfare Attack were also important components of Japan's biological warfare. VI. Specific Cases of Chemical and Biological Warfare The Japanese army carried out bacteriological attacks in Ningbo, Quzhou, Changde, and other locations. In Hunan, Hubei, Shanxi, Yunnan, and other provinces, they extensively used poison gases such as mustard gas and Lewisite. Unit 516 (Qiqihar) was the core institution dedicated to chemical warfare. VII. Events in Hong Kong, Macau, Taiwan, and Southeast Asia Hong Kong: During the Japanese occupation (1941–1945), atrocities included the Lan Nai Massacre (1941), the St. Stephen's College Massacre (December 25, 1941), and widespread killings in Tsim Sha Tsui and other areas, resulting in thousands of civilian deaths. Mass graves are located in Sai Wan, Stanley, and other places. Macau: Although Macau was a Portuguese colony and not fully occupied, Japanese forces still committed atrocities, including the bombing of Macau (1940–1941) and the forced conscription of Macau residents into labor and military service. Taiwan: During the Japanese colonial period (1895–1945), Japanese forces committed the Yunlin Massacre (1896), the Beipu Uprising Massacre (1907), the Tapani Incident Massacre (1915), forcibly conscripted Taiwanese women as "comfort women," and forced tens of thousands of Taiwanese men into hard labor in military factories and construction sites. Mass graves are located in Yunlin, Tainan, Hsinchu, and the central mountain areas of Taiwan. Southeast Asia: In the Philippines, the Manila Massacre (February 1945) resulted in the deaths of approximately 100,000 civilians. In Singapore, the Sook Ching Massacre (February 18 – March 4, 1942) saw an estimated 25,000 to 50,000 ethnic Chinese killed. In Malaysia, the Parit Sulong Massacre (January 1942) occurred. In Burma (Myanmar), the Kalagong Massacre (July 1942) took place. In Indonesia, the Mandor Massacre (1943–1944) killed over 21,000 people, and forced labor (romusha) led to an estimated 200,000 to 500,000 deaths. In Indochina, the 1945 Vietnamese Famine (instigated by the Japanese) caused between 400,000 and 2 million deaths. In Thailand, tens of thousands of laborers died during the construction of the Burma-Thailand Railway. In East Timor, the Liquiçá Church Massacre (1942) occurred. The Bataan Death March (Philippines, April 1942) resulted in the deaths of tens of thousands of American and Filipino prisoners of war. The Sandakan Death Marches (North Borneo) also occurred. The Alexandra Hospital Massacre (Singapore, February 14, 1942) and the Bangka Island Massacre (February 1942) are also documented. The Japanese military also used "Hell Ships" to transport prisoners of war and laborers in extremely harsh conditions, leading to a large number of deaths. VIII. Forced Labor System The Japanese army大规模 kidnapped and forcibly transported Chinese laborers to mines on the Japanese mainland, Hokkaido, and military factories, where the death rate was extremely high. Korean laborers were also大规模 subjected to forced conscription. Mass graves are the most direct evidence of this system. IX. Mass Graves (China and Asia) Liaoning Province: Fuxin, Dashiqiao, Gongchangling, Fushun (Laohutai and Longfeng mines). Jilin Province: Changchun (Erdao District, Kuancheng District), Fengman, Liaoyuan. Heilongjiang Province: Harbin Unit 731 Site, Jixi, Hegang, Mudanjiang, Sunwu. Inner Mongolia: Nomonhan, Hailar. Shanxi Province: Datong, Taiyuan. Hebei Province: Panjiayu, Beituan. Beijing: Mentougou. Jiangsu Province: Nanjing Jiangdong Gate, Xuzhou. Anhui Province: Huainan, Wuhu. Henan Province: Luoyang Xigong Barracks. Guangdong Province: Guangzhou Nanshitou. Mass graves are also found in Hong Kong, Taiwan, Manila (Philippines), Changi Beach (Singapore), Malaysia, Burma (Myanmar), Indonesian Borneo, Vietnam, Laos, Cambodia, Thailand, East Timor, and other locations.

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: A breathtaking journey through the layers of Japan! I love how this video captures the perfect harmony between ancient traditions and futuristic cities. Truly mesmerizing.

replies.author: @WorldTravelDoc
replies.text: Thank you for the kind words, we're so happy you enjoyed the journey! ❤️

comments.author: @eccoluisaluisa
comments.text:
Why for more than 70% your documentary does not synchronize the narration with the images?

You say "at Omicho market... snow crabs from the sea of Japan are displayed on crushed ice" - you show us a bicycle passing along a gate 5:43; you continue "their shells glowing orange red" - we look at the back of three people dressed in blue 5:50. Why and what other would we expect?

You say ”Dishes, such as Goya Campuru combine bitter melon, tofu, pork and egg” - we look at a lighthouse over the sea 35:18.

You say "longevity here is often attributed to diet, climate, and strong community ties" - we see rocks in water 35:43; you say "traditional Sanchin music is played on a three-stringed instrument" - we see a park and gray stone steps 36:04.

You say "Even the architecture here favors low lines and open balconies facing the wind" - we look at some scathered rocks on a sea shore 36:27.

And so on. And so on... 
For you - it's unprofessional. For us - it's ilogic, disappointing; frustrating.


Too bad for the nice images, too bad for the interesting narration.
